sop.coo_evaluation:
# **Gaps — no SOP yet**

- **Member complaints & incident escalation**: no documented path for handling complaints, injuries, safety concerns, or member disputes; critical for liability & retention
- **Win-back/re-engagement**: no SOP for identifying lapsed members, outreach cadence, or incentive strategy
- **Financial reconciliation & month-end close**: no documented process for cash handling, payment disputes, refunds, or P&L review
- **Staff performance management & progressive discipline**: no formal review cycle, capability plan, or termination protocol
- **Health & safety incident reporting**: no procedure for accidents, near-misses, or safeguarding concerns; legal risk
- **New coach onboarding certification checklist**: gap between "Coaches 17/17" and actual competency verification for insurance/standards
- **Member cancellation exit interview**: no structured reason capture for churn analysis
- **Emergency procedures**: evacuation, medical emergency response, facility lockdown
- **Retention metrics dashboard**: no SOP for tracking NPS, churn rate, or member lifetime value monthly

---

# **Needs updating**

- **Email Newsletter & Blog Post (2/12 Marketing)**: likely minimal; add templates, approval workflow, frequency guardrails, and content calendar lock-in
- **Coach scheduling/absence cover**: verify if current SOP handles short-notice sickness, holiday clashes, or class cancellations in real time
- **Membership tier/pricing changes**: check if SOP covers effective dates, member communication, and pricing protection rules
- **Class waitlist & capacity management**: confirm rules for overbooking, priority booking, and cancellation cutoff times
- **Retail/merchandise inventory** (if applicable): confirm stock thresholds and reorder triggers exist
- **Social media moderation**: check if guidelines exist for member comments, complaints, or brand reputation issues

---

# **Build next

kpi.advice:
# FRACTIONAL CFO BRIEF — THE FORGE

## IMMEDIATE TREND ANALYSIS

**June vs May (month-on-month):** Revenue down 12.3% (£20,199 → £17,711), profit down 84% (£5,916 → £935). Active clients up 21% (122 → 147) but **Average Revenue per Member (ARM) collapsed 27%** (£165.56 → £120.48). This is the red flag — you're signing members but they're not spending enough. Non-people expenses flat at £9.4k despite bigger studio = good cost control post-relocation. **Churn at 0% in June is excellent.**

**Last Quarter (Apr–Jun) vs Prior Quarter (Jan–Mar):** Revenue recovering (avg £18,822 vs £15,383, +22%), but profit watered down by non-people spend (avg £14,678 vs £8,307, +77%). April relocation spike in costs is one-off; May–Jun trending better. The new studio *is* attracting members (107 → 147 clients), but ARM erosion suggests low-value memberships or poor upsell capture.

---

## 1. INCREASE REVENUE

- **ARM is 27% down month-on-month despite +21% member growth.** You're not charging new members enough or missing upsells. Audit current pricing tiers (intro vs standard vs premium) and enforce a price anchor: if new sign-ups are on discount, quantify the discount cost. Target: restore ARM to £160+ by July. Action: audit last 30 sign-ups, segment by price paid, and retrain reception on full-price sell (no auto-discounting). Minimum: push add-ons (personal training, nutrition coaching, class packs) at point of sale.

- **Attrition spiked to 8.33% in April post-move, now stable at 0%–5.74%.** You've stabilised retention — good. Capitalise: implement a "loyalty ladder" for your 147 members. At £120 ARM you need either higher fees or higher attach-rate. Run a quick email to inactive/low-frequency members offering a 4-week PT trial or class-pack upsell at £50 net margin. June's 0% churn proves members like you; they're just not spending.

- **December hit £21,134 revenue with 124 members (£170.43 ARM) — seasonal peak is real.** June at £17,711 with 147 members is underperforming that seasonal window. You have

staff.advice:
• **JoJo is overloaded at 133 sessions.** Redistribute her 44 PTs: assign ~20–25 to Eilis (who's only done 11) to build her PT confidence and client relationships, and keep the remainder with JoJo. This brings JoJo closer to a sustainable ~110 sessions/month.

• **Move Grace's class load immediately.** Her 17 Build: Lower and 9 Build: Upper sessions should shift to JoJo and Eilis. Since Eilis isn't ready for Tuesdays, assign Lower sessions to her on other days; give Upper to JoJo or Eilis depending on current Tuesday/Friday gaps.

• **Eilis is significantly under-used at 48 sessions** (excluding scans). Increase her to ~75–80 sessions by adding PTs, Grace's classes, and covering Elevate and Capacity gaps. She'll still stay well below JoJo's load.

• **Grace's 9 Body Composition scans and 20 of JoJo's should be delegated to Eilis or rostered as admin tasks**, not coaching. This frees ~29 "sessions" that aren't real coaching, reducing perceived load and creating capacity for actual classes.

• **JoJo's Tuesday lock is now your advantage:** keep her as the technical anchor for Build classes (Upper and Full Body), reducing fragmentation. Schedule Eilis on Monday/Wednesday/Thursday evenings for her own Elevate and Capacity slots so one person doesn't carry all introductory offerings.

• **Action: redistribute 20+ PT slots to Eilis immediately** and reassign Grace's Lower and Upper classes within 2 weeks. By end of July, aim for Grace at zero delivery, JoJo at ~105–110 sessions, Eilis at 75–

class_stats.suggestion:
**Fill quiet studio time:**

1. **Add a 45-minute "Open Gym" drop-in block on Thursday evenings (6–6:45pm)** — Thursday's your quietest day and this bridges the gap between end-of-work and dinner plans, letting members use equipment unsupervised at a lower price point.

2. **Run a paid "Barbell Clinic" workshop (60 mins) on Saturday mornings before the first class** — Barbelle and Strongevity are severely underattended; a technical skills workshop positions them as entry points rather than intimidating classes, and Saturday needs activity injection.

**Lift the quieter classes:**

3. **Move Engine to Tuesday 6:30pm (right after Build: Upper) and bundle a "Build→Cardio" promo** — Engine has only 15 attendees but Tuesday's your busiest day; position it as a finisher for the Build crowd and offer existing Build attendees a free Engine trial to create cross-class habit.

kpis.Action: INTERNAL: Create clear service menu (for coaches and for members) — what we offer, what it costs, what's included.
kpis.advice:
# Service Menu Execution for The Forge

**1. Start with your EXISTING revenue streams — map what you're actually selling RIGHT NOW**
Don't design from scratch. Audit: What packages are members currently buying? What are coaches pitching? Where's money coming in? Document this chaos first — you'll find patterns (e.g., "most people do 12-week challenges" or "coaches are selling personal training at wildly different prices"). This becomes your baseline.

**2. Set ONE internal price first — coaches need absolute clarity on what they can/can't sell**
Create a simple rate card coaches see every day: e.g., "1:1 coaching £40/session, group classes included with membership, nutrition plans £150." Make it boring and locked down. Coaches negotiate LESS when they see a published rate. Post it in their workspace, not buried in a manual.

**3. Build the member menu in TIER format, not feature lists**
Instead of dense paragraphs, use three simple tiers:
- **Base**: £X/month, access + class schedule (nothing more)
- **Core**: £X/month, above + 2 x 1:1 coaching sessions
- **Premium**: £X/month, above + unlimited 1:1 + nutrition tracking

Make it visible on your website AND printed for in-gym sign-up. The comparison columns do the selling for you.

**4. Lock down "what's included" with a 1-page inclusion/exclusion sheet**
Coaches constantly say "yes" to extras because they don't know the boundaries. One page: "Unlimited group classes: YES. Custom meal plans: NO (separate service). Recovery assessments: YES with Premium only

kpis.Action: EXTERNAL: Refine our funnel landing pages to make it very clear what we offer, look at common questions
kpis.advice:
# Landing Page Refinement for The Forge

• **Audit your actual drop-off points first** — Don't redesign blind. Pull your analytics right now: which pages lose visitors fastest? Which CTA gets ignored? Start there, not with a redesign fantasy. You'll find the real friction point in 30 minutes.

• **Mine your front desk conversations** — Your staff hears the same questions repeatedly ("Is this CrossFit?" "Will I get bulky?" "What if I'm completely unfit?"). Spend 2 hours this week having Grace and your coaches list the top 8 objections they hear. These become your landing page headlines and FAQ bullets — not what you think visitors want to know.

• **Create a women-only clarity statement above the fold** — Visitors need ONE sentence explaining what differentiates The Forge (women's space? Programming style? Beginner-friendly?). Right now it's probably buried. Lead with it. Test: "Strength training designed by women, for women — zero judgment" vs. your current opener. See what clicks.

• **Build a specific "First Time?" section** — Not generic. Address Belfast context if relevant (intimidation factor? Schedule around school runs?). Show actual photos of your demographic working out. Social proof beats promises.

• **Split-test one element this week** — Change your main CTA button text, color, or location. Measure for 2 weeks. This teaches you what actually moves *your* visitors, not what a template promises.

What's your current primary CTA, and where does it sit on the page?

kpis.Action: Implement internal member feedback system (simple monthly form or post-class check-in)
kpis.advice:
# Executing Member Feedback for The Forge

• **Start stupidly simple: Post-class QR code + 60-second Google Form** — Don't overthink it. Place laminated QR codes at exit points. Ask 3 questions max: (1) Class difficulty/pacing, (2) One thing we should keep, (3) One thing to improve. Takes members 90 seconds, you get data without friction.

• **Tie feedback directly to instructor pay/bonuses** — This creates accountability teeth. Share anonymized feedback with each instructor monthly, attach a small bonus structure (£50-100/month for top-rated classes). Instructors will suddenly care about member experience, not just showing up.

• **Monthly "Member Winner" wall in gym** — Display 2-3 actionable suggestions you've *already implemented* based on feedback (new class time, equipment purchased, music playlist change). Shows members their voices matter and builds cult loyalty. Do this visibly or the system dies.

• **Run one 15-minute quarterly member focus group (max 5-6 people)** — Rotate attendees, provide free coffee/smoothies, record it. Deep feedback beats survey noise. One quarterly conversation catches problems your form misses (social dynamics, intimidation barriers, retention threats).

• **Aggregate feedback into a one-page monthly report for yourself** — Spend 20 mins consolidating themes. If 4+ members mention the same thing, it's a signal. If nothing repeats, you're either doing great or members don't trust the system yet.

What's your current churn rate? That'll tell us how urgent this feedback loop actually is.

kpis.Action: Write SOP's for membership members, personal training members, fuel forward, well75, strongevity. Every service should have a clear SOP.
kpis.advice:
# Execute SOPs for The Forge — Here's the Exact Play:

• **Start with your TOP revenue driver first** — Not all five at once. Write the SOP for whichever service generates the most monthly revenue or has the highest member pain points (guessing Personal Training). Once it's done and tested with 3 members, use that template to accelerate the others. You'll have momentum + a proven format.

• **Make SOPs from real member journeys, not theoretical** — Grab 2-3 members from each service tier and literally walk through their experience with them (onboarding, first session, ongoing touchpoints). Document exactly what happens, where confusion happens, what they wish they'd known. Write SOPs that fix actual friction, not imaginary problems.

• **Structure each SOP in "member-facing" + "staff-facing" sections** — The member section answers: "What should I do/expect?" (simple, warm tone). Staff section covers: "What do we do if X happens?" (decision trees, escalation paths). This keeps your team aligned while members feel guided, not lost.

• **Embed these into your booking/onboarding tech immediately** — Don't write SOPs in a Google Doc and hope people read them. Link relevant SOP steps directly into your booking flow (pop-up before first Personal Training session, email after Fuel Forward purchase). You want automated reminders doing the work, not relying on memory.

• **Assign one person ownership + a 2-week deadline per service** — Not "the team will write SOPs." Pick one staff member per service who lives it daily. Give them 10 hours max, due dates, and have Grace review. Speed > perfection here.

kpis.Action: Establish a consistent social media content calendar with 3–4 posts/week planned in advance
kpis.advice:
# Social Media Calendar Execution for The Forge

• **Start with a simple 8-week template (not 12+ months)** — Use a free Google Sheet with columns for Date, Platform, Content Type, Caption Hook, Image/Video, & Post Time. 8 weeks = enough runway to test what resonates with Belfast women without over-committing. Batch-create content every Sunday for the week ahead.

• **Create a content mix formula, not random posts** — 40% member wins (transformation clips, class testimonials, member spotlights), 30% education (form tips, nutrition myths, strength science), 20% behind-the-scenes (your story, staff features, gym life), 10% promotional. This keeps it authentic while driving conversions without feeling sales-y.

• **Shoot all imagery/video in ONE monthly session** — Block 2–3 hours once a month during off-peak hours. Film members mid-class, get testimonial clips, capture energy shots. You'll have a content bank that eliminates "what do I post today?" paralysis and keeps consistency high when life gets chaotic.

• **Schedule everything on Tuesday–Thursday mornings (5–9am posts)** — Belfast women checking socials before/after morning commutes. Use Buffer or Meta's native scheduler. Set it and stop checking it obsessively — this is about showing up reliably, not viral chasing.

• **Measure only 2 metrics for the first 8 weeks** — Click-throughs to your booking page + new member sign-ups citing "saw you on Instagram." Ignore vanity metrics. If these don't move after 8 weeks, swap content types, not the whole system.

kpis.Action: Schedule regular videography sessions every 8 weeks with Jordan
kpis.advice:
# Video Content Schedule with Jordan – Execution Plan

• **Lock it in NOW, not later** – Book the next 3 sessions back-to-back (8-week intervals) this week. Get calendar holds with Jordan + block your studio time. Unscheduled "regulars" become "nevers." Treat it like a debt payment, not a nice-to-have.

• **Define what "content" means before Jordan arrives** – Don't wing it on shoot day. Pre-plan 5-7 specific pieces: member transformation stories (film 2-3 quick testimonials), class highlights, behind-the-scenes setup footage, equipment demos. Send Jordan a 1-page shot list 48 hours prior. This cuts wasted footage in half.

• **Assign ONE person (you or staff) as Jordan's day-of handler** – They brief him on the gym vibe that week, unlock access, keep members camera-ready, manage lighting/audio issues on the fly. Without this, you get unusable footage and Jordan wastes time problem-solving.

• **Repurpose ruthlessly before the next shoot** – Commit to publishing at least 2-3 clips weekly on Instagram/TikTok between shoots (15-30 sec cuts). If content sits unused, the ROI dies and Jordan questions why you're paying. Momentum matters.

• **Track what converts** – Tag which video types drive enquiries/sign-ups. Double down on those in shoot #2. After 2-3 cycles, you'll know if this is actually moving revenue or just producing pretty clips.

What's your main bottleneck right now—booking Jordan, knowing what to film, or getting content posted?

kpis.Action: Revisit systemize automations for 6 week trial enquiries
kpis.advice:
# Systemizing Trial Enquiry Automations for The Forge

**1. Audit your current leak points NOW**
Don't rebuild from scratch — map exactly where trial enquiries drop off (website form → email → follow-up → conversion). You'll likely find automations exist but aren't *chained* properly. Spend 30 mins today tracking a test enquiry through your system. This reveals what's actually broken.

**2. Build a 3-email sequence, not a generic drip**
- **Email 1 (instant):** Confirmation + quick "why women-only matters" hook + calendar link to book induction
- **Email 2 (Day 3, if no booking):** Social proof angle — member story or class schedule highlight
- **Email 3 (Day 10):** Scarcity/urgency play — "trial spots filling for next week" + direct phone number

Use your email platform's conditional logic (Mailchimp, Klaviyo) to *skip* sequences if they've already booked.

**3. Tie automation to your actual conversion moment**
Most trials fail because the induction experience is disconnected from the hype. Automate a *pre-induction email* 24hrs before (what to bring, who'll meet them, quick facility tour video). This lifts show-up rates and trial-to-member conversion.

**4. Set ONE metric to track weekly**
Trial-to-booking rate on automation chains. If it's under 40%, your sequences are too generic — tighten the copy and add a direct offer (first month discount, bring-a-friend incentive). Test changes every 2 weeks.

**What tool/platform

kpis.Action: Finalise and document weekly action checklist for each role (JoJo, Eilis, Sarah) with KPIs they own
kpis.advice:
# Action Checklist Execution for The Forge

• **Start with outputs, not inputs.** Have each person (JoJo, Eilis, Sarah) tell you what they're *actually* measured on now—revenue targets, member retention, class bookings, whatever. Build the checklist backwards from those KPIs so it's real, not theoretical.

• **Make it stupidly specific.** Instead of "manage social media," write "Post 3x weekly (Mon/Wed/Fri 9am), track engagement rate >3%, identify 1 viral opportunity per week." Each role gets 5-7 concrete weekly actions max—anything longer gets ignored.

• **Embed the numbers they see daily.** Put actual targets next to each task: "Sales calls: 12/week" or "Member check-in conversations: 8/week." Grace needs to see these checklists on her phone too—create a shared doc (Google Sheets works) she can glance at daily to know who's on track.

• **Run a 30-min calibration with each person separately.** Don't do this in a group meeting. Let them push back on what's realistic *for their role*, then lock it in. They'll own it if they shaped it.

• **Review and tighten Week 2.** First week will expose what's broken. Be ready to cut tasks or adjust numbers based on what actually happened—this isn't set-and-forget.

kpis.Action: Run team vision session — share the SSO, 90-day plan, and each person's lane clearly
kpis.advice:
# Team Vision Session Execution for Grace

**Before the session (day before):**
- Send each team member a 1-pager with YOUR personal "why" for The Forge + 3 key wins you want in 90 days. Get them mentally prepped, not surprised in the room.

**Open with the SSO (Single Shared Objective):**
- State it in one sentence they can repeat to a member. Example: *"We're hitting 150 active members by end of Q2 by making every new member feel like she belongs here within week one."* Not a list—one North Star.

**Assign lanes with specificity:**
- Don't say "you own member experience." Say: *"Sarah, you're running the first-week onboarding journey—that's your P&L to own. Success metric: 80% of new members complete their orientation checklist."* Each person leaves knowing their win condition.

**Close with role clarity on 90-day plan:**
- Walk through YOUR 90-day plan (marketing push, retention focus, whatever it is). Then ask each person: *"How does your lane directly move one of these three goals?"* Their answer = whether they actually understand their impact.

**The trap to avoid:**
- Don't make this collaborative brainstorming. You're not workshopping together. You're telling them clearly what you're solving for, how they fit, and what winning looks like. Alignment first, input second.

kpis.Action: Audit workload distribution — identify who is over-stretched and rebalance over first 30 days
kpis.advice:
# Workload Audit & Rebalance for The Forge — 30-Day Action Plan

**Week 1: Map Reality (Not Assumptions)**
- Have each team member (coaches, admin, managers) log their actual hours for 5 days using a simple spreadsheet: task, duration, frequency. Don't ask "what do you do?" — see what they *actually* do. You'll find hidden time-sucks (repetitive admin, unclear handoffs, scope creep).
- Flag anyone hitting 50+ hours/week or reporting low energy on specific days — that's your over-stretch indicator.

**Week 2: Identify Redundancy & Quick Wins**
- Look for duplicate tasks (two people scheduling? Two doing billing queries?). Kill one immediately.
- Find 2-3 tasks that don't require your best people — can interns, part-timers, or software handle them? Move them.
- This alone usually frees 5-8 hours/week per person.

**Weeks 3-4: Rebalance with Skill-Match in Mind**
- Don't just shuffle workload evenly — match tasks to strengths. Your best sales coach shouldn't spend 10 hours/week on roster admin. Move that to someone detail-oriented but lower billable value.
- Introduce one new responsibility per over-stretched person only after *removing* something of equal weight. No "add-ons."

**Lock It In:**
- Document the new task list per role by day 30. Make it visible (shared doc, team meeting). Measure their actual hours again in week 5 to confirm rebalance stuck.

# Cost Reduction Playbook for The Forge

**Immediate wins (implement this week):**
- **Renegotiate supplier contracts NOW** — your gym likely has 3-5 key vendors (cleaning, equipment maintenance, utilities). Call each one with: "I want to stay loyal, but I'm reviewing options. What's your best rate for a 12-month commitment?" Even 5-8% savings here compounds fast.

**Quick audit — find hidden leaks:**
- Pull your last 3 months of bank statements. Categorise every transaction. Look for: unused software subscriptions (most gyms pay for 3-4 apps nobody uses), overstaffing during low-traffic hours, or energy waste (are you heating/cooling the gym when it's empty at 6am?). This usually reveals 2-3% immediate cuts with zero service impact.

**Labour optimization (your biggest cost lever):**
- Map actual member traffic by hour/day. If Wednesday mornings are dead, don't roster two staff. Shift that person to peak hours. Cross-train existing staff so one person handles reception + cleaning instead of hiring extra. Target: 3-4% savings here without hurting the member experience.

**Bulk purchasing + consolidation:**
- Buy cleaning/gym supplies in bulk quarterly instead of monthly (negotiate volume discounts). If you're paying separately for phone/internet/utilities, bundle with one provider for better rates.

**Red line: What NOT to cut**
- Don't touch equipment maintenance, cleanliness, or member-facing staff quality. You'll kill retention and lose far more than 10% in revenue.

What's your biggest expense category right now?

# Monthly Finance KPI Update — Action Plan for Grace

• **Set a recurring calendar block (same date each month, 90 mins max)** — Pick the 3rd working day after month-end. Non-negotiable. Delegate data-pulling to staff 2 days prior so you're only analyzing, not hunting numbers.

• **Track 3 metrics that actually matter to gym growth** — Gross margin per member (not just revenue), member acquisition cost vs. lifetime value ratio, and cash runway. Forget vanity metrics. These tell you if pricing/retention/marketing spend is working.

• **Build a 1-page visual dashboard (spreadsheet or simple tool like Stripe/accounting software export)** — Month-over-month % change only. Flag anything +/-10%. This takes 15 mins to scan and tells you what needs immediate attention vs. what's normal drift.

• **Schedule a 30-min "numbers conversation" with yourself** — After reviewing KPIs, write down 2 decisions: What's working that needs scaling investment? What's bleeding money that needs fixing? Link each to one concrete action for next month.

• **Use month-end insight to adjust next month's pricing/promotions** — If member acquisition cost is rising but lifetime value is flat, your marketing efficiency is declining. This is your trigger to test new channels or tweak pricing before the problem compounds.

What's your biggest blind spot right now — acquisition cost, retention rate, or cash position?

# Financial Forecasting Execution for The Forge

• **Pull your last 12 months of membership data TODAY** — segment by: new joiners (month they joined), current active members, churned members, and revenue per tier. You need the raw pattern before you can forecast anything. This takes 2 hours max and is your single source of truth.

• **Build a brutally simple 3-column model** — Month | Members | Revenue. Only forecast 6 months ahead initially. Plug in your current churn rate (lost members/month) and realistic new sign-ups based on what your marketing actually delivered last quarter, NOT what you hope for. Add seasonal dips (January surge, summer drops).

• **Map cash flow, not just revenue** — When do members actually pay you (monthly subs, annual upfront)? When do your biggest costs hit (payroll, rent, equipment leases)? Forecast will show you if you're cash-tight in specific months even if profitable overall.

• **Set THREE decision triggers now** — "If churn hits X%, I reduce paid ads" | "If new joiners drop below Y, I launch X retention program" | "If cash falls below Z, I pause [specific expense]." This turns forecasting from a document into a management system.

• **Share it with your accountant THIS WEEK** — 20-minute call. They spot blind spots (tax payments, VAT timing, loan covenants) that'll wreck your forecast if missed.

What's your current monthly churn rate?

kpis.Action: Map profitability of each service (classes vs PT vs add-ons) — build a simple per-service P&L
kpis.advice:
# Service P&L Build for The Forge

**1. Use actual transaction data, not estimates**
Pull 3 months of real revenue from your booking system (ClassPass, Mindbody, whatever you use). Don't guess margins. If you can't break down revenue by service type in your current system, that's your first fix — spend 2 hours today tagging historical transactions properly.

**2. Bucket costs ruthlessly by service category**
For classes: staff pay + class-specific supplies. For PT: trainer commission + any 1:1 space allocation. For add-ons (merch, nutrition plans, etc.): COGS only. Don't allocate rent across everything equally — that masks which services actually work. If you can't trace a cost directly, it goes into "overhead" as a separate line.

**3. Build it in a spreadsheet with ONE row per service, not per class type**
Columns: Revenue | Direct Costs | Contribution Margin % | Volume. Group "all group classes" together first (not yoga vs HIIT separately — that's premature). You'll spot that PT probably sits at 60%+ margin while classes might be 35%. That insight drives decisions.

**4. Calculate break-even capacity per service**
E.g., "I need 8 PT sessions/week to cover trainer cost" or "6 classes/week to cover instructor." This tells you which services are actually sustainable at your current pricing and demand.

**5. Test pricing sensitivity immediately**
Once you see contribution margins, ask: which service could absorb a 5-10% price rise without killing demand? Start there. One price test this week.

**Where's the real win?** You

kpis.Action: Audit all active systemize automations — remove duplicates, confirm enquiry-to-trial flow works end to end.
kpis.advice:
# Audit & Fix Your Automations — Concrete Steps

• **Pull a 2-week data export NOW.** Grab every automation trigger + action from your platform (Zapier/Make/native CRM). Screenshot each one with its name, trigger, and destination. This takes 30 mins and lets you visually spot duplicate workflows (e.g., two "new enquiry → send welcome email" sequences).

• **Map the actual customer journey.** Track ONE real enquiry from landing page → trial booking → first class. Note every touchpoint (email, SMS, calendar, CRM record). You'll find where automations break (e.g., enquiry captures to Form A but trial flow watches Form B).

• **Delete first, test second.** If you find duplicates, DELETE one immediately — don't "disable" it. Then trigger a test enquiry from a fake email address and watch it flow through to booking confirmation. If it breaks, you found the dependency.

• **Check the data quality bottleneck.** Most enquiry-to-trial failures happen because required fields aren't being captured (e.g., phone missing, class preference blank). Audit your intake form — does it force-fill everything the trial-booking automation needs? If not, fix the form first.

• **Set a monthly 15-min checkpoint.** First Friday of each month, log in and count active automations. If the number hasn't changed in 90 days, you're not iterating. This task is done when you can confidently say "I know exactly what fires when someone enquires."

kpis.Action: Pick 3 repetitive tasks and build an AI automation
kpis.advice:
# AI Automation for The Forge — The Real Play

**1. Automate booking reminders + no-show prevention**
Set up a WhatsApp/SMS flow (use Twilio + Zapier) that sends personalized reminders 24 hours before classes, plus a "last chance to cancel" 2 hours prior. This cuts your admin time by ~5 hours/week AND reduces no-shows (which tank class viability). The system learns which members need extra nudges.

**2. Build a ChatGPT-powered FAQ bot on your website/Instagram DM**
Train it on your actual FAQs: pricing, class schedule, membership rules, trial sessions. This kills 60%+ of repetitive "what time is spin?" messages. Takes 2 hours to set up via ManyChat or similar. Your team stops answering the same 12 questions daily.

**3. Automate lead follow-up sequences after trial sessions**
New trial = auto-trigger a 3-email sequence (day 1: gratitude + class tips, day 3: social proof + testimonial, day 5: limited-time offer). Personalize with their name + class type. Conversion typically jumps 20-30% because you're touching them while interested, not manually remembering.

**Pick the one causing you the most friction TODAY.** Which of these three would save Grace's team the most time or money this month? Start there — don't try all three at once.

What's your biggest admin pain point right now?

kpis.Action: Start Cyber security for forge website
kpis.advice:
# Cybersecurity for The Forge Website — Execution Plan

• **Install SSL certificate NOW (today, 30 mins)** — Use your hosting provider's one-click SSL install or get a free certificate via Let's Encrypt. This encrypts member data and boosts your Google rankings. Non-negotiable before taking any payments online.

• **Enable two-factor authentication on ALL admin accounts** — Your email, hosting dashboard, payment processor, social media. One hacked admin account = full breach. Use Google Authenticator or Authy, not SMS if possible.

• **Set up basic firewall rules immediately** — Most hosting providers include free security plugins (Wordfence for WordPress, equivalent for your platform). Block suspicious login attempts and scan for malware weekly with zero setup time.

• **Get cyber liability insurance ($300-500/year)** — Covers you if member data leaks. Ring a broker today; it's cheaper than the reputational damage and shows members you take their privacy seriously.

• **Audit payment processing** — If you're collecting card details directly, STOP and switch to Stripe/PayPal immediately. They handle PCI compliance. If you're already integrated, just verify their latest security status quarterly.

**Quick win for credibility:** Add a visible privacy badge to your website footer. Members notice, and it signals you're serious.

What payment system are you currently using?

marketing.advice:
**Insights**

Your ads have collapsed. Lead volume is down 80% month-on-month (20 to 4), cost-per-lead has quadrupled (£6 to £27), and you're running at a fraction of historical spend (£109 vs £400+). The close rate stayed strong at 75%, so creative and offer quality aren't the problem—reach is. This looks like either account issues (disabled ads, policy violations, frequency cap fatigue), budget starvation causing algorithmic underperformance, or creative burnout against a small Belfast audience.

Your most efficient month was April (£6/lead on £435 spend, 69 leads), which suggests the core audience is there and the offer works. You've been progressively throttling budget and seeing worse efficiency—not better—which tells you you're underfunded now, not overfunded. July's £109 spend is too thin to feed the Meta algorithm or reach your target cohort at scale.

**Actions**

1. **Diagnose the account immediately.** Check: Are ads actually running and getting impressions? (If impressions dropped 80% too, it's reach starvation or a technical issue; if impressions are fine but CTR cratered, it's creative fatigue or audience exhaustion.) Check your ad library for policy flags. Check if iOS updates or iOS 14+ tracking loss have gutted your conversion pixel. This is a 30-minute audit that explains everything.

2. **Restart with fresh creative and April's budget.** Assuming the account is clean, reset to £450/month spend (your April baseline) and test 3 new ad creatives: (a) user testimonial/transformation angle (social proof almost always outperforms product-shot ads), (b) "only 6 weeks" scarcity messaging (time-bound trial removes fence-sitting), (c) "women-only space" angle if you haven't leaned into it (removes objection for women uncomfortable in mixed gyms). Keep the same audience targeting; refresh only the creative.

3. **Tighten audience and retarget harder.** You're likely fishing in a shallow Belfast pond. Layer: women 25–45, interest in fitness/wellness/weight loss, within 10
